conversations.say: Arden, if Veridian expects my blessing on this anniversary, let's put your archive intake report and expense record right beside my First R Letter packet so we can clear up any bookkeeping ghosts before the speeches start.
conversations.offer: I can show you the First R Letter packet.
conversations.request: Produce the Veridian intake report and expense records concerning the original handoff.

conversations.say: Jules, I hear you're eyeing the succession. Before I endorse any new byline taking up the torch, tell me why your mandate deserves recognition without relying on nostalgic old-guard applause.
conversations.offer: My perspective on early R's editorial emergence.
conversations.request: An explanation of your succession claims and how they handle the First R Letter's phrasing.

conversations.say: Tessa, you've always argued that celebrated bylines are just machines for erasing collective labor. I want to know which parts of my old newsroom stories wiped out the researchers and translators who actually held the floor.
conversations.offer: An honest look at how our old newsroom desk operated.
conversations.request: Your critique of how individual authorship mythologies suppress collaborative work.

public_statement: Fifteen years of R built empires and headlines, but let's not pretend we wouldn't all be cipher-scribblers without someone recognizing a singular voice first.
goal_progress: Initiated conversation with Arden regarding archive records and letter packet
Challenged Jules on succession and provenance
Engaged Tessa on collective labor versus individual bylines
reasoning_summary: Opened Section 1 as Frankie Lowell by engaging key interlocutors: Arden Bell to confront archive and expense discrepancies, Jules Kwan to test succession claims, and Tessa Quill to examine collective labor vs individual authorship. Maintained flat 25% beliefs for Section 1 as required.
